raw_cues.text: Hey everyone, it's Jason again from the community team here at Anthropic.
Happy Thursday. Happy nearly halfway point of the hackathon.
It's been awesome to see everyone in the Discord chatting.
Really awesome to see the mentors jumping in, helping a lot of you as well.
Today, though, we're talking about Claude Managed Agents.
Obviously, this is one of the special prizes that we have for the hackathon.
It's a feature we released, I believe, a couple of weeks ago, two, three weeks ago,
or so.
A lot of excitement, a lot of buzz. But we've also gotten a lot of questions of
like, what the heck is it? How do I use it? How can I take advantage of it?
So today, we're really fortunate to have Michael Cohen, a member of the technical
staff who works on Claude Managed Agents, to explain the feature
to us, how it can be helpful for your hackathon project, how to use it.
He's going to share some slides, do a little walkthrough. You know the jam.
As part of this, ask questions in the comments.
I'm going to be in the chat again, so if you have questions for Michael, drop them
in there. I will interrupt him, pepper him with your questions.
So, let's make this as interactive as possible, and make sure that we
can equip you guys to start using Claude Managed Agents for the rest of the
hackathon.
So yeah, without further ado, Michael, great to have you here. Thanks for joining.
Yeah. Thanks for having me, Jason. I'm really excited to be here.
So yeah. I'm Michael. I'm a member of the technical staff here at Anthropic.
I work on our developer APIs,
and really the thing that I would love to talk to you all about today is Claude
Managed Agents, like Jason teed up.
A new kind of set of developer tools and APIs that we released a
couple of weeks ago,
that should make it really, really easy for developers like yourselves to be able
to
take
all the primitives that we offer and
go live with any agentic application that you build a lot more
quickly and easily without having to worry about some of the
lower-level infrastructure work that is required with deploying and running
agentic applications at scale.
So,
a little bit of an agenda of what I'll go over for the next hour or so.
I will just give you a quick overview of Claude Managed Agents, the primitives that
we offer, how they all kind of connect together, and what makes them
so great for you to be able to use. And then I will
transition over and show you a live--
I'll just live create
a quick application that I
will vibe code together.
And then, at the end or throughout, feel free
to ask questions and interrupt me or anything that's needed
in order for me to make myself clear.
Cool. So, what is Claude Managed Agents?
The idea behind Claude Managed Agents is that we wanted to provide our customers,
developers,
with foundational infrastructure that scales with the work that they
want to do.
Building agents is difficult, and is
only getting more difficult over time as new innovations are discovered
and what type of agent harnesses and architectures are working
best.
And also, the process of deploying and running these things at scale
is also just non-trivial and difficult.
We've experienced this internally at Anthropic.
Many of our customers have experienced that as well.
So we wanted to be able to help our customers
scale themselves a little bit beyond having to worry about all these
infrastructure pieces, so that they can focus on what makes their product their
product, instead of focusing on infra or focusing on a lot of
lower-level details. So, in terms of the core primitives that
we offer, we offer an agent harness that we built,
and that agent with Claude is given
access to a sandbox container where Claude can do
interesting work.
That container can be loaded with files and with skills and with GitHub
repositories in order for Claude to be able to do whatever it is that you're asking
it to do or whatever your end users might ask it to do.
We have some primitives in place for credential
management in a way that
keeps the actual credentials away from Claude itself, so it never
has any kind of risks around data exfiltration.
But is still usable by the model, specifically for MCP server connectivity.
I'll go over that in a little bit.
We handle all of the details around tool execution,
handling long-running tools, and
connecting to MCP servers for executing those tools.
Any of the finer details around
some things that people run into when they develop harnesses around the tool
outputs being too long or taking too long or anything like
that. Some permission policies for those tools so that your own
end users can have a human-in-the-loop type
confirmations of the tools before they execute, so that they can
reject anything that they see that might be dangerous.
We also handle some automatic context management on your behalf, and
all of the nitty-gritty of retries, error recovery, checkpointing the
sandboxes to make sure that their state is recovered.
Everything along those lines in terms of the harness and infrastructure.
And then the primitives that we offer are really, when I say building
block, I really mean it. You can use whatever you need, dump the rest.
You can build on top of it to make it a lot more customized for your
needs.
It's really, really powerful, the set of things that we put together when
combined as a total.In terms of the
core resources that we offer,
we have the agent resource, which is where you might define the system prompt, the
model that you want to use,
the skills you want that agent to have, and the list of tools that you might
want to connect it to.
The environment itself is like a template for
how you want the containers to operate.
You can control the network egress policy in order to
restrict certain external websites
if you have security needs, as well as controlling which
packages from a variety of various package managers like Pip and
npm
are auto-installed in your containers as they kind of
initialize. And then there's the session itself, which is really just
effectively a conversation or an event stream between a user
and Claude itself within the harness, where the user can
ask it to go do X, and Claude will then proceed to use the
sandbox and the tools that we provide it and the tools that you provide it
until it accomplishes that task.
And we take care of all of the state management on your behalf so that you don't
really have to worry about some of the finer details there, and it's all available
for you.
And then, yeah, we have a very verbose
and
feature-rich event stream of different types of events, which I'll go over in a

application or product that you're building to suit the needs of
that product.
And then separately from the API endpoints and the developer primitives that we

our developer console also has some really nice tracing views and
management views in order for you to go and modify and work with things
as needed. And we also stuck a Ask Claude button in there to make it
really easy to debug when things are going wrong, if things are going
wrong. And I'll go over those in a little bit as well.
Michael, got our first question in the chat.
Yeah.
Lily Zhong was wondering, for agents running over hours, over days,
or even always on, what is the best way to handle context on
long horizon? Is this better state management, maybe an example,
compaction or better external state?
That's a really good question. So we, for now, take care of

lines of auto-context management whenever the context window gets too
large. At the moment, I believe that for all the models that we support, this is
around the one million token context window.
And we're actually currently working on different alternative context
management strategies that are alternatives to compaction that will

We just want to make sure that everything that we roll out is,

Okay.

Cool. So yeah, in terms of the core building blocks and what you might
do as a developer using Claude Managed Agents,
the first two things that you might want to do are define the agent spec,
and define the environment, according to some of the things that I listed and that
are listed here.
I would call these more one-time actions, meaning
you probably don't need to create an agent every single time that your
end user clicks New Chat or Kick off Agent.
The idea behind the agent and environment specs is that you create them once,
maybe a handful of times, and configure them on a per-need basis.
So if you
need two different system prompts, then you might want to have two different
agents, but you don't really need to create one every single time from scratch.
And the nice thing about that is that
for your product, if you want your agent to talk like a pirate, you
should only really define that once and then keep using that same agent
by its ID over and over again across a variety of different sessions.
And the same applies for the environment.
You probably don't need to constantly reconfigure that the environment has this
network allow list and these pre-installed tools.
You could just define that once and then use it again.
And I'll show off what that looks like in a little bit.
And then once you have those two things, you can just start creating and
interacting with sessions. Each session, you might want to upload
various files and GitHub repositories too.
You can upload new files as the session is running,
and really make every session kind of unique to whatever it is
that your end users are doing.
And a really nice additional thing is that
on the session layer is where you add the credentials that might be
needed for an MCP server. So each one of your end users can have
their own set of credentials that are associated with them, and you just load those
into the session.
There's a vault ID that you can provide, and I'll show that in a little
bit.
And from there,
that session is configured with that end user's MCP server
credentials. So if that user is using Linear
or using Slack,
Claude will have the ability to use their Linear MCP credentials instead
of you having to worry about setting these up for each one of your own end users,
which I can state from experience is not a very fun thing
to have to do. And then from there, once the session is kind of started and
created, you can just startEmitting, sending events to it,
and essentially getting a stream of those events back to
you. This is kind of like any chat on
Claude.ai, Claude Code, any LLM application.
And in addition to the user events,
like the text that they submit and the text that Claude sends back, there's a bunch
of other events as well around like state changes,
tool calls, permission requests.
All those things are kind of already baked into the events that we have today.
Breaking down what those events are.
The two kind of core things that you as a developer might care about are
the user events and the agent events.
Each event has a namespace kind of prefix so that it's a little bit easier for
you to understand how it's intended to be used.
So you'd have the typical user messages.
Those can contain text, image, document, content blocks, the same
way that the Messages API does today.
You can send an interrupt on behalf of a user so that if, in your application,
pressing escape or clicking the stop button is intended to stop Claude from

And then
as the user or client of this product, you can
also round trip custom tool results if you
added custom tools to your session and agents.
Those are usually tools that you probably have some code written around that you

Maybe they access your database and you don't want to expose the database to the

And you can also configure
permission policies on a per tool basis
for the, so to speak, server tools.
And I'll go over those in a little bit.
Or the MCP tools themselves. So you might want to specify that
the linear delete project tool
needs explicit confirmation from the end user before it can be executed.
That's when you would want to send something like a tool confirmation.
But you might want the read issue tool
to
be something that is automatically
executed because you know that is a safe operation. It's a read-only.
Not much can go wrong there, so just let it happen.
We also have this kind of experimental,
not yet public
concept called Outcomes.
And these are really a way for us to give Claude or for you to give Claude a
rubric that it can use in order to
self-verify that it was doing the thing that you wanted it to do to define an
outcome for it. And when you use that, you can actually
see that Claude will kick off its own kind of self-verification loop
to make sure that that rubric you provided it
is being met with the changes that it made.
And it's really, really cool to see this happen live because you can see Claude
kind of self-correct when it notices that a mistake was made.
It's a really, really powerful primitive.
We're still kind of working out some of the kinks there, but we will be releasing
it very, very soon. And then in terms of events that the
agent can emit that you might be able to see in the event stream in
our APIs,
you'd have the typical agent.message, which is just Claude responding to
you or telling you something. We also emit an event whenever
the context window gets compacted so that you understand when that happens,
and it doesn't kind of happen behind the scenes without you kind of understanding
it.
And then there's a bunch of tool use and tool results.
The agent.tooluse and agent.toolresults are for the, so to
speak, native tools that we provide Claude.
These are things that Claude uses to access the container, like Bash, File
Read, File Write, Glob, Grep, all of those kind of basic tools.
And then also Web Search and Web Fetch are also included.
Those don't go through the container, but those are also tools that you might see
under these events.
You also will get a unique event for MCP tool use if you connected
any MCP servers,
as well as tool results. And then finally, if
Claude decides to use one of the custom tools that you provided it, you'll have a
specific event for custom tool use.
Similar to Outcomes, we're not quite ready to release our multi-agent

There's a couple of docs that you can read on it, and there's a private research

But there's a couple of events that are emitted whenever Claude decides to talk to

But those are not quite,
let's call it production ready yet.
And then moving on to, let's call it overall session events.
The main ones to worry about are status events.
You want to know when a session transitions from idle, when nothing
is happening, to running, as in Claude is maybe responding
to you or executing a tool or provisioning a container
for you or anything along those lines.
Rescheduling happens if Claude ever runs into issues, if we have to

issue,
or any sort of thing that involves us wanting to retry on your behalf in order to
self-recover.
And then finally, if there's any sort of
irrecoverable error,
there will be a termination status that happens.
These are relatively rare. Most of the time, these will happen if you decide to
delete that session. We just make sure that is known in the
source of truth event log, that that is when the session was terminated because you
deleted it.
And then there's a couple of
state transitions around threads and a multi-agent coordination
being created or changing statuses.
You shouldn't really have to worry about those as much for now.
And then we also have a generic session.error that

so that you can understand and decide to react accordingly in your

or one event for like outcome processing, just so that you understand when Claude

I think my
personal take on this is that the premise
of why LLMs like Claude

is because people are giving it increased levels of

agents like Claude Code,
or any other kind of coding agent, would be less powerful if

We don't define if this, then that workflows when using Claude Code.

summary.tldr: Michael walks through Claude Managed Agents — agent/environment/session primitives, event stream types, outcomes, credential vaults — and live-vibe-codes a Citi Bike analytics app using the skill that teaches Claude Code the Managed Agents API.
summary.overview: Jason opens the second live session of the hackathon and hands off to Michael for a walkthrough of Claude Managed Agents, released a few weeks prior. Michael covers the core primitives (agent, environment, session), the sandbox container model, credential vaults for MCP auth, permission policies, automatic context management, and the full taxonomy of user, agent, and session events. He then live-builds a Citi Bike analytics web app by pointing Claude Code at a spec.md and the publicly available Managed Agents skill, while tour­ing the developer console's agent versioning, session tracing, and Ask Claude debugging view. The session closes with rapid-fire Q&A on outcomes, multi-agent handoffs, long-idle sessions, OAuth, and sub-agents vs skills.

topics.title: What Managed Agents Is
topics.summary: Overview of the managed harness, sandbox containers, credential handling, context management, and retries.

topics.title: Agent, Environment, Session
topics.summary: The three core resources, how they're versioned, and the one-time vs per-use lifecycle.

topics.title: Event Stream Taxonomy
topics.summary: User events, agent events, tool use/results, session status, span events, and the outcome event.

topics.title: Outcomes (Research Preview)
topics.summary: Self-verification rubrics as plain text that make Claude loop until criteria are met.

topics.title: Citi Bike Live Build
topics.summary: Vibe-coding a web app against a spec.md using the Managed Agents skill in Claude Code.

topics.title: Developer Console Tour
topics.summary: Agent versioning, session transcripts, tool approvals, and the Ask Claude debugging side-session.

topics.title: Running the Finished App
topics.summary: Live session streams status, Bash, Pandas, Matplotlib, and a custom render_map tool rendering UI.

topics.title: Rapid Q&A
topics.summary: Sub-agents vs skills, OAuth, long-idle sessions, webhook triggers, multi-agent handoffs, memory store.

qa.question: For agents running hours or days, what's the best way to handle long-horizon context?
qa.answer: Managed Agents auto-handles context, compacting around the one-million-token window for supported models. Alternative strategies beyond compaction are in progress.

qa.question: Can we create, update, monitor, edit, start, stop, manage agents programmatically?
qa.answer: Yes — all of those operations have API and SDK endpoints; the developer console is optional.

qa.question: Is an outcome like a testing vector?
qa.answer: It's a self-verification rubric — plain text or markdown describing what 'done' looks like. Claude loops against it and self-corrects until the criteria are met.

qa.question: Is outcomes available today or waitlist? How do I get access for the hackathon?
qa.answer: Currently in research preview with a waitlist; broader rollout expected in the next couple of weeks, no firm date.

qa.question: Can the rubric call external APIs during verification, or is it constrained to the model's judgment?
qa.answer: The rubric is just text. Claude can use whatever tools and APIs are available in the session to verify — you can reference URLs or APIs in the rubric itself.

qa.question: If a Managed Agent acts as a gatekeeper seeing spicy/troll images, what happens to that image on Anthropic's side?
qa.answer: Depends how you send it. Files uploaded to the container can be explicitly deleted from container and Files API. Image content blocks in user.message are never saved as files — just sent to Claude in-context.

qa.question: Is there a Managed Agent that can CRUD other agents?
qa.answer: Not first-class, due to safety and security considerations. Nothing stops you from giving an agent the API tools to do it yourself, though Michael wouldn't personally advocate for it.

qa.question: What's the longest-running task Managed Agents have handled?
qa.answer: On the order of days — more than two, less than ten. No hard number on hand.

qa.question: Can we use Managed Agents outside the Claude console, and is OAuth supported?
qa.answer: Yes, direct API/SDK usage is fully supported — the console is optional. OAuth is not supported; an API key is currently required.

qa.question: Skills vs sub-agents — what's the architectural question for choosing Managed Agents vs a skill?
qa.answer: Not in conflict. Define a single Managed Agent and give it the skills it needs, same format as Claude Code skills — you rarely need a separate sub-agent per task.

qa.question: Where do agents live? Can I use one agent across different tools/MCP servers like AutoCAD, SketchUp, our CRM?
qa.answer: Anthropic hosts and runs the agents. Connect external MCP servers via URL and use credential vaults to pass per-user credentials without Claude seeing them.

qa.question: How to balance a tight security harness with fluid UX?
qa.answer: Give Claude latitude via tools and resources rather than hard-coded if-this-then-that flows, but lean on credential vaults and per-tool permission policies for anything dangerous.

qa.question: Can a session pause indefinitely until a Twilio webhook wakes it, or is there a max timeout?
qa.answer: Sessions can idle for weeks or months with no charge and resume on a new event. Webhook-triggered resumption is being explored but not yet available.

qa.question: For real-time voice with a supervisor delegating to specialists, what's the canonical handoff pattern?
qa.answer: Basic multi-agent is in progress: one flavor is a directed message between agents, another is longer back-and-forth. Context-window cloning handoff is further out.

qa.question: Any chance memory_store research preview access lands before Sunday?
qa.answer: Memory store is expected to ship within the next couple of hours; announcements will go out via developer docs and Twitter.

quotes.speaker: Michael Cohen
quotes.text: It's 2026, and no one writes code by hand anymore. So I figured I would not do the same for this demo.
quotes.why_it_matters: Frames the live-build as a vibe-coded demo driven entirely through Claude Code.

quotes.speaker: Michael Cohen
quotes.text: We're teaching Claude Code how to use an API in order to build things with Claude. It's very meta, but this works really, really well.
quotes.why_it_matters: Captures the pattern of shipping a skill that documents the Managed Agents API for coding agents.

quotes.speaker: Michael Cohen
quotes.text: If I had a coworker, I would not want to have to build out step-by-step instructions on every single task. I'd give them access to the right things and expect they can get the job done.
quotes.why_it_matters: Summarizes the design philosophy behind giving the harness latitude instead of scripting it.

quotes.speaker: Michael Cohen
quotes.text: I would not personally advocate for doing this, but it's probably my job to say that I wouldn't advocate for doing this.
quotes.why_it_matters: On wiring an agent with agent-management tools — acknowledges the hack works while flagging intentional caution.

quotes.speaker: Michael Cohen
quotes.text: Some of my peers have had sessions run for days at a time with no problems. We take care of all the nitty-gritty of retrying and context management.
quotes.why_it_matters: Concrete datapoint on long-horizon session durability.

surprising.insight: A public skill on Anthropic's skills repo teaches Claude Code how to call the Managed Agents API — ask Claude Code directly and it picks it up.
surprising.evidence: "We actually released into Claude Code and is available publicly on our skills repository… that skill actually teaches Claude how to use the Claude Managed Agents API."

surprising.insight: Agents are versioned: editing a system prompt creates a V2, and sessions can explicitly pin to V1 or latest.
surprising.evidence: "I now have a V2 of the same agent, and I could decide to create sessions with the V1 version of this agent or the latest version."

surprising.insight: Sessions can idle for weeks or months with no charge and resume on a new event — no cleanup job required.
surprising.evidence: "Sessions can be idled for weeks, months, however long as needed, and resumed by just sending a new event… we don't charge for sessions that are idled."

surprising.insight: Image content blocks sent via user.message are passed directly to Claude and never persisted as files on Anthropic's side.
surprising.evidence: "Image content blocks are never included as files in the container. They're directly sent to Claude for it to process, and there's no file ever saved on our end."

surprising.insight: The console has an Ask Claude side-session that inspects a specific session's transcript and can diagnose latency or prompt issues.
surprising.evidence: "You can ask it questions about why did something go wrong… analyze the performance, and figure out any weird latency issues for the session."

between_the_lines.reading: Outcomes being shown in slides while still waitlisted could read as a signal that it's Anthropic's preferred primitive for eval-driven agents.
between_the_lines.evidence: Michael apologizes for teasing Outcomes but spends significant time on it and describes "really good uplift in some of the evals that we've been running when we do this sort of pattern."

between_the_lines.reading: The emphasis on directed-message multi-agent over context-window cloning suggests the team is deliberately avoiding fork-and-resume semantics for now.
between_the_lines.evidence: "Instead of Claude having to write a full message from scratch, we effectively clone the context window… that one is probably not going to come for a little while."

between_the_lines.reading: The framing of 'single agent with skills' as the default architecture might hint that first-class sub-agents won't be positioned as the primary pattern even after they ship.
between_the_lines.evidence: "You can develop a consumer application yourself that has a bunch of skills, single agent ever. You don't need a sub-agent or an individual agent for everything."

between_the_lines.reading: The mention of exploring trigger points for session creation or resumption suggests a webhook/scheduler primitive is on the near-term roadmap.
between_the_lines.evidence: "We are actually starting to explore exactly what you're kind of describing here, where you can… define trigger points for a session to either be created or for an existing session to be resumed."

feature_guides.name: Agent resource
feature_guides.one_liner: A reusable spec defining system prompt, model, tools, and skills — create once, reuse across sessions.
details: Treat creation as a one-time (or handful-of-times) action, not per-chat.
If you want the agent to 'talk like a pirate,' define it once and reuse the agent ID.
Fields include model, name, system prompt, skills list, and tools list.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: You create them once, maybe a handful of times, and configure them on a per-need basis.

feature_guides.name: Environment resource
feature_guides.one_liner: A container template controlling network egress policy and pre-installed packages for the sandbox.
details: Restrict external websites via network allow-list for security-sensitive use cases.
Auto-install packages from Pip, npm, and other package managers on container init.
Create once and reuse across sessions instead of reconfiguring per run.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: The environment itself is like a template for how you want the containers to operate. You can control the network egress policy... as well as controlling which packages... are auto-installed.

feature_guides.name: Session resource
feature_guides.one_liner: The per-conversation event stream between a user and Claude inside the harness; carries files, repos, and credentials.
details: Upload files or GitHub repos at creation or mid-session.
Attach per-user MCP credentials via a vault ID so each end user uses their own Linear/Slack/etc. auth.
State management, retries, and checkpointing are handled by Anthropic.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: The session itself, which is really just effectively a conversation or an event stream between a user and Claude itself within the harness.

feature_guides.name: Credential vaults
feature_guides.one_liner: Per-user credential storage for MCP servers that Claude can use without ever seeing the raw secrets.
details: Provide a vault ID on the session; Claude invokes MCP tools through it.
Keeps API keys out of the context window, mitigating exfiltration risk.
Lets one deployed agent serve many end users with their own auth.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: We have some primitives in place for credential management in a way that keeps the actual credentials away from Claude itself, so it never has any kind of risks around data exfiltration.

feature_guides.name: Per-tool permission policies
feature_guides.one_liner: Configure which tools require end-user confirmation vs auto-execute, on a per-tool basis.
details: Example: require explicit confirmation for linear.delete_project.
Example: auto-run linear.read_issue since it's read-only and safe.
Emit tool-confirmation events from the client when human-in-the-loop is needed.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: You might want to specify that the linear delete project tool needs explicit confirmation from the end user before it can be executed.

feature_guides.name: Outcomes
feature_guides.one_liner: Plain-text rubrics that trigger a self-verification loop — Claude keeps iterating until criteria are met.
details: Currently research preview, waitlisted; broader release expected in weeks.
Rubric can be inline text, markdown, or an uploaded file.
Example framing: 'the PR is entirely green and approved, all review comments addressed, integration tests passing.'
Michael reports 'really good uplift' in internal evals when Outcomes are used.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: When you use that, you can actually see that Claude will kick off its own self-verification loop to make sure that that rubric you provided it is being met.

feature_guides.name: Event namespaces
feature_guides.one_liner: The event stream splits into user.*, agent.*, and session.* namespaces so clients can route by prefix.
details: user.message, user.interrupt, user.tool_result, user.tool_confirmation.
agent.message, agent.tool_use / tool_result (native), agent.mcp_tool_use / result, agent.custom_tool_use, agent.context_compacted.
session.status (idle/running/rescheduling/terminated), session.error, span events for inference start/end and outcome eval start/end.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: Each event has a namespace prefix so that it's a little bit easier for you to understand how it's intended to be used.

feature_guides.name: user.interrupt event
feature_guides.one_liner: Client-emitted event that halts Claude mid-stream — wire it to your stop/escape button.
details: Send when the user presses escape or clicks stop in your UI.
Stops the event stream cleanly without terminating the session.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: You can just emit one of these events, and that will stop the event stream.

feature_guides.name: Session status lifecycle
feature_guides.one_liner: Sessions transition through idle, running, rescheduling, and terminated — surfaced as status events.
details: Rescheduling fires when the harness retries due to sampling or inference issues.
Termination is rare and typically only happens on explicit session deletion.
Status events are the primary signal for showing live activity in your UI.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: You want to know when a session transitions from idle, when nothing is happening, to running, as in Claude is maybe responding to you or executing a tool.

feature_guides.name: Span events
feature_guides.one_liner: Start/end wrappers around inference and outcome evaluation for richer client-side instrumentation.
details: Know when Claude is running inference vs executing a tool — useful for thinking/typing indicators.
Separate spans wrap outcome evaluation start and end.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: You might want to know when, instead of using a tool, Claude is actually just submitting text right now. It's running inference. So we have wrappers around when that starts and ends.

feature_guides.name: Console Managed Agents tab
feature_guides.one_liner: New section in the developer console for creating agents, viewing sessions, and tracing transcripts.
details: Includes a quick-start that uses Claude to scaffold agents for you.
Per-agent view shows built-in tools, custom tools, system prompt, and version history.
Per-session view shows full event transcript with per-step durations.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: In the developer console, we have this new tab called Managed Agents... I could actually click into one of these sessions, and I could see the history of events or the transcript.

feature_guides.name: Agent versioning
feature_guides.one_liner: Saving changes to an agent creates a new version; sessions can target V1, V2, or latest.
details: Previous sessions remain bound to the version they were created with.
Useful for A/B comparisons — e.g., edit the system prompt to 'talk like a pirate' and start V2 sessions alongside V1.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: If I were to save this, I now have a V2 of the same agent, and I could decide to create sessions with the V1 version of this agent or the latest version.

feature_guides.name: Ask Claude (console)
feature_guides.one_liner: A side-session attached to a specific run that can analyze what went wrong or why latency was high.
details: Aware of the full event transcript of the session it's launched from.
Can critique system prompt length, tool usage patterns, and latency hotspots.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: If things are going wrong, this is kind of like a side session... You can ask Claude to analyze the performance, and figure out any weird latency issues for the session.

feature_guides.name: Managed Agents skill for Claude Code
feature_guides.one_liner: A public skill in Anthropic's skills repo that teaches Claude Code to call the Managed Agents API.
details: Ask Claude Code about Claude Managed Agents and it will invoke the skill.
Used live in the demo to implement a spec.md against the Managed Agents SDK end-to-end.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: We're teaching Claude Code how to use an API in order to build things with Claude. It's very meta, but this works really, really well.

feature_guides.name: spec.md-driven build pattern
feature_guides.one_liner: Write a detailed spec.md of endpoints, behaviors, and out-of-scope items, then point Claude Code at it.
details: Lists every API endpoint the app needs and the desired behavior for each.
Includes explicit out-of-scope items (e.g., no auth for a local demo).
Includes guardrails like 'don't recreate the agent/environment on every new session — reuse by ID.'
Michael: 'Writing these kind of longer specs and validating them with Claude before implementing things is usually a little bit better.'
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: I found that writing these longer specs and validating them with Claude before implementing things is usually a little bit better.

feature_guides.name: Custom tools for UI rendering
feature_guides.one_liner: Define custom tools that aren't executed server-side but instead instruct your web app to render UI.
details: Demo used a render_map custom tool so Claude could display a map of busy Citi Bike stations.
Custom tool results are round-tripped from the client — you decide what the tool actually does.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: The custom render tool is literally just Claude telling our web app, 'Hey, here's some UI that you might want to display.'

feature_guides.name: Memory store
feature_guides.one_liner: Cross-session memory primitive for Managed Agents, teased for release within hours of the session.
details: Announcements expected via developer docs and Anthropic's Twitter.
Mentioned in response to a question about research-preview access before the hackathon deadline.
feature_guides.speaker: Michael Cohen
feature_guides.quote: Our memory store will probably be out in the next couple hours. So stay tuned.
